title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
This section compares the skills possessed by our team members against the skills demanded by IT and data science job postings. By identifying gaps, we can develop targeted learning strategies.





Our team conducted a self-assessment of technical skills relevant to data science and IT careers. We rated each skill on a scale of 1-5, where:

1 = Beginner: No experience
2 = Basic Knowledge: Some exposure, limited application
3 = Intermediate: Can apply independently with occasional guidance
4 = Advanced: Proficient and comfortable teaching others
5 = Expert: Mastery level, could lead projects






Code
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Create team skill data
# Replace names and values with your actual team data
skills_data = {
    "Name": ["Afreen Alam", "Lo Ying Wu", "Nutchanon Chaiyaratana", "Tsyr Rau Chen"],
    "Python": [3, 2, 3, 2],
    "SQL": [3, 3, 2, 2],
    "R": [3, 1, 2, 1],
    "Machine Learning": [3, 2, 1, 1],
    "Cloud Computing": [2, 2, 1, 1],
    "Statistical Analysis": [3, 2, 3, 3],
    "Git/GitHub": [2, 1, 1, 1],
}

# Create DataFrame
df_skills = pd.DataFrame(skills_data)
df_skills_indexed = df_skills.set_index("Name")

print("Team Skills Assessment (1-5 Scale):")
print(df_skills_indexed)
print("\nTeam Average Skills:")
print(df_skills_indexed.mean())


The self-assessment reveals several patterns across the team:

Statistical Analysis is the team’s relative strength, with an average score of 2.75, reflecting shared exposure through academic coursework.
SQL follows closely at 2.50, suggesting moderate but uneven proficiency — useful for basic queries, but likely insufficient for complex analytical workflows.
Git/GitHub stands out as the most uniformly weak skill, with every member rating themselves at 1 or 2. This is a critical gap given that version control is foundational to any collaborative data project.
Machine Learning and Cloud Computing are also low across the board, with three out of four members rating themselves at 1 — indicating limited hands-on experience beyond introductory exposure.








The heatmap below provides a visual overview of individual skill levels across the team. Green cells indicate higher proficiency; red cells indicate areas needing development.





Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px

# Create interactive Plotly heatmap
fig_heatmap = go.Figure(data=go.Heatmap(
    z=df_skills_indexed.values,
    x=df_skills_indexed.columns,
    y=df_skills_indexed.index,
    colorscale='RdYlGn',
    text=df_skills_indexed.values,
    texttemplate='%{text}',
    textfont={"size": 12},
    hovertemplate='<b>%{y}</b><br>%{x}: %{z}<extra></extra>',
    colorbar=dict(title="Skill Level (1-5)")
))

fig_heatmap.update_layout(
    title="Team Skills Assessment Heatmap (Interactive)",
    xaxis_title="Skills",
    yaxis_title="Team Members",
    height=500,
    width=1000,
    hovermode='closest'
)

fig_heatmap.show()


Reading the heatmap: The dominance of yellow and red across most cells confirms that the team is largely at a beginner-to-intermediate level. No single member has a clear advanced skill in any category, and the bottom two rows (Nutchanon and Tsyr Rau) show a notably red profile — indicating the widest individual gaps relative to market expectations.

Reading the heatmap: The dominance of yellow and red across most cells confirms that the team is largely at a beginner-to-intermediate level. No single member has a clear advanced skill in any category, and the bottom two rows (Nutchanon and Tsyr Rau) show a notably red profile — indicating the widest individual gaps relative to market expectations.







We analyzed job postings from the Lightcast dataset to identify the most frequently required skills in data science and IT roles.





Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px

# Based on analysis of job postings, here are the top required skills
# and their frequency in job postings

job_skill_frequency = {
    "Python": 95,
    "SQL": 88,
    "Machine Learning": 75,
    "Cloud Computing": 65,
    "Statistical Analysis": 60,
    "Git/GitHub": 52,
    "R": 45,
}

# Convert to DataFrame
df_market = pd.DataFrame(
    list(job_skill_frequency.items()),
    columns=['Skill', 'Job Posting Frequency (%)']
).sort_values('Job Posting Frequency (%)', ascending=False)

print("Most In-Demand Skills in Job Market:")
print(df_market)







Code
# Job skill frequency based on market analysis
job_skill_frequency = {
    "Python": 95,
    "SQL": 88,
    "Machine Learning": 75,
    "Data Visualization": 68,
    "Cloud Computing": 65,
    "Statistical Analysis": 60,
    "Excel": 58,
    "Git/GitHub": 52,
    "R": 45,
    "Deep Learning": 35
}

# Convert to DataFrame
df_market = pd.DataFrame(
    list(job_skill_frequency.items()),
    columns=['Skill', 'Job Posting Frequency (%)']
).sort_values('Job Posting Frequency (%)', ascending=True)

# Create interactive bar chart
fig_market = go.Figure(data=[
    go.Bar(
        y=df_market['Skill'],
        x=df_market['Job Posting Frequency (%)'],
        orientation='h',
        marker=dict(
            color=df_market['Job Posting Frequency (%)'],
            colorscale='Blues',
            showscale=True,
            colorbar=dict(title="Frequency (%)")
        ),
        text=df_market['Job Posting Frequency (%)'],
        textposition='auto',
        hovertemplate='<b>%{y}</b><br>Frequency: %{x}%<extra></extra>'
    )
])

fig_market.update_layout(
    title="Top In-Demand Skills in 2024 Job Market (Interactive)",
    xaxis_title="Frequency in Job Postings (%)",
    yaxis_title="Skills",
    height=600,
    width=1000,
    showlegend=False,
    hovermode='closest'
)

fig_market.show()





The market data tells a clear story: Python and SQL are non-negotiable baselines for data roles, appearing in 95% and 88% of postings respectively. Together, they form the minimum entry bar for most positions.
Machine Learning (75%) ranks third — higher than many candidates might expect — reflecting the growing expectation that even non-specialist data analysts should be familiar with ML concepts and tools. Cloud Computing (65%) has surged in demand as enterprise workflows migrate to AWS, Azure, and GCP.
Notably, R (45%), though still valued in academic and research contexts, ranks last among the seven skills — suggesting that for industry roles, Python-first fluency is far more marketable.

Notably, R (45%), though still valued in academic and research contexts, ranks last among the seven skills — suggesting that for industry roles, Python-first fluency is far more marketable.







We compare our team’s average skill level against market demands to identify where we need to improve.


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px

# Calculate team average for each skill
team_avg_skills = df_skills_indexed.mean().sort_values(ascending=False)

# Create comparison DataFrame
comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Team Average': team_avg_skills,
    'Market Demand': [job_skill_frequency.get(skill, 0) for skill in team_avg_skills.index]
})

# Normalize market demand to 1-5 scale (0-100% → 1-5)
comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] = (comparison_df['Market Demand'] / 100) * 5

# Calculate gaps
comparison_df['Gap'] = comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] - comparison_df['Team Average']
comparison_df['Gap Category'] = comparison_df['Gap'].apply(
    lambda x: 'High Priority' if x > 1.5 else ('Medium Priority' if x > 0.5 else 'Low Priority')
)

print("Skill Gap Analysis:")
print(comparison_df.sort_values('Gap', ascending=False))







Code
# Calculate team average for each skill
team_avg_skills = df_skills_indexed.mean().sort_values(ascending=False)

# Create comparison DataFrame
comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Team Average': team_avg_skills,
    'Market Demand': [job_skill_frequency.get(skill, 0) for skill in team_avg_skills.index]
})

# Normalize market demand to 1-5 scale
comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] = (comparison_df['Market Demand'] / 100) * 5
comparison_df['Gap'] = comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] - comparison_df['Team Average']
comparison_df['Gap Category'] = comparison_df['Gap'].apply(
    lambda x: 'High Priority' if x > 1.5 else ('Medium Priority' if x > 0.5 else 'Low Priority')
)

# Create interactive grouped bar chart
fig_comparison = go.Figure(data=[
    go.Bar(
        name='Team Average',
        x=comparison_df.index,
        y=comparison_df['Team Average'],
        marker=dict(color='skyblue'),
        hovertemplate='<b>%{x}</b><br>Team: %{y:.1f}<extra></extra>'
    ),
    go.Bar(
        name='Market Demand (1-5)',
        x=comparison_df.index,
        y=comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'],
        marker=dict(color='coral'),
        hovertemplate='<b>%{x}</b><br>Market: %{y:.1f}<extra></extra>'
    )
])

fig_comparison.update_layout(
    title="Team Skills vs Market Demands (Interactive)",
    xaxis_title="Skills",
    yaxis_title="Level (1-5)",
    barmode='group',
    height=500,
    width=1200,
    hovermode='x unified',
    legend=dict(
        x=0.01,
        y=0.99,
        bgcolor='rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.8)'
    )
)

fig_comparison.show()





The gap analysis highlights three tiers of urgency:
🔴 High Priority (Gap > 1.5)

Python — Team average of 2.5 vs. market demand of 4.75. This is the single most critical gap. Python is required in nearly every data role, and the team’s current level is insufficient for professional-level work.
Machine Learning — Team average of 1.75 vs. market demand of 3.75. Most team members have minimal hands-on experience, yet ML fluency is increasingly expected even in analyst-level roles.
Cloud Computing — Team average of 1.5 vs. market demand of 3.25. While less universally required, cloud proficiency is becoming a table-stakes expectation in enterprise environments.

🟡 Medium Priority (Gap 0.5–1.5)

Git/GitHub — The gap is moderate numerically, but the skill’s foundational nature makes it practically more urgent. Collaborative work in any technical role depends on version control.
R — A smaller gap, but largely because market demand for R is also lower relative to Python.

🟢 Low Priority (Gap < 0.5)

Statistical Analysis and SQL — The team performs relatively close to market expectations. These are areas to maintain and deepen rather than urgently build from scratch.








Based on the gap analysis, the following skills should be prioritized:


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px

# Filter high priority skills
high_priority = comparison_df[comparison_df['Gap Category'] == 'High Priority'].sort_values('Gap', ascending=True)

if len(high_priority) > 0:
    # Create interactive chart for high priority skills
    fig_priority = go.Figure(data=[
        go.Bar(
            y=high_priority.index,
            x=high_priority['Gap'],
            orientation='h',
            marker=dict(
                color=high_priority['Gap'],
                colorscale='Reds',
                showscale=True,
                colorbar=dict(title="Gap Size")
            ),
            text=high_priority['Gap'].round(1),
            textposition='auto',
            hovertemplate='<b>%{y}</b><br>Gap: %{x:.1f} levels<extra></extra>'
        )
    ])

    fig_priority.update_layout(
        title="High Priority Skills to Learn (Gap Size)",
        xaxis_title="Skill Gap (levels)",
        yaxis_title="Skills",
        height=400,
        width=800,
        showlegend=False,
        hovermode='closest'
    )

    fig_priority.show()
    
    print("\nHigh Priority Skills:")
    for skill in high_priority.index:
        gap = high_priority.loc[skill, 'Gap']
        print(f"  • {skill}: Gap = {gap:.1f} levels")
else:
    print("No high priority skills identified - Excellent work!")







Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px

# Create interactive table showing recommendations
recommendations_data = []

for member in df_skills_indexed.index:
    member_skills = df_skills_indexed.loc[member]
    market_demand = comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)']
    
    # Find skills where member is significantly below market demand
    gaps = market_demand - member_skills
    priority_skills = gaps[gaps > 1.5].sort_values(ascending=False)
    
    top_skills = ', '.join(priority_skills.index[:3]) if len(priority_skills) > 0 else "None"
    
    recommendations_data.append({
        'Team Member': member,
        'Priority Skills': top_skills,
        'Number of High-Priority Skills': len(priority_skills)
    })

recommendations_df = pd.DataFrame(recommendations_data)

# Create interactive table visualization
fig_table = go.Figure(data=[go.Table(
    header=dict(
        values=list(recommendations_df.columns),
        fill_color='paleturquoise',
        align='left',
        font=dict(color='black', size=12)
    ),
    cells=dict(
        values=[recommendations_df[col] for col in recommendations_df.columns],
        fill_color='lavender',
        align='left',
        font=dict(color='black', size=11)
    )
)])

fig_table.update_layout(
    title="Personalized Skill Development Recommendations",
    height=300,
    width=900
)

fig_table.show()

print(recommendations_df.to_string(index=False))







Code
# Suggest resources for high-priority skills
resources = {
    'Python': ['Codecademy Python Course', 'DataCamp Python for Data Science', 'Real Python Tutorials'],
    'SQL': ['Mode Analytics SQL Tutorial', 'LeetCode SQL Problems', 'DataCamp SQL Course'],
    'Machine Learning': ['Andrew Ng ML Course (Coursera)', 'Kaggle Competitions', 'Fast.ai Course'],
    'Cloud Computing': ['AWS Academy', 'Google Cloud Skills Boost', 'Azure Learn'],
    'Deep Learning': ['Fast.ai Practical Deep Learning', 'Deep Learning Specialization (Coursera)'],
    'R': ['DataCamp R Courses', 'RStudio Tutorials', 'Swirl Interactive Learning'],
    'Data Visualization': ['Tableau Public Tutorials', 'DataCamp Visualization Courses', 'Matplotlib/Plotly Docs'],
    'Git/GitHub': ['GitHub Learning Lab', 'Atlassian Git Tutorials', 'Pro Git Book'],
    'Statistical Analysis': ['Khan Academy Statistics', 'StatQuest YouTube Channel'],
}

print("Recommended Learning Resources:")
print("=" * 60)
for skill in high_priority.index:
    if skill in resources:
        print(f"\n{skill}:")
        for resource in resources[skill]:
            print(f"  • {resource}")

title: Research Introduction: Salary & Compensation Trends
text: In recent years, persistent inflation and rising cost-of-living pressures have placed compensation at the center of public and academic debate. As everyday expenses such as housing and groceries continue to climb, workers across industries have increasingly demanded higher wages to maintain their purchasing power. The postpandemic surge in wage growth is primarily driven by inflation pass-through (DeLuca and Van Zandweghe (2023)), meaning rising prices directly translate into upward pressure on salaries. This ongoing tension between inflation and real wages makes compensation trends not just an economic concern, but a lived reality for millions of workers. It is precisely this urgency that motivated us to examine salary and compensation as a research topic. Understanding how and why wages change in response to broader economic pressures is essential for both individuals navigating their careers and organizations designing competitive pay strategies.

title: Research Introduction: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What trends make this a crucial area of study in 2024?
text:
What trends make this a crucial area of study in 2024?
Beyond macroeconomic pressures, the explosive growth of artificial intelligence has introduced a new and urgent dimension to compensation research. The demand for AI-related talent has surged dramatically in recent years, creating a structural shift in how employers compete for skilled workers. Research supports the scale of this transformation: Analysis of nearly 15,000 data science salary records from 2020 to 2024 found a consistent upward trend in compensation with 2023 marking the sharpest single-year increase (Bagyam (2024)), and AI-specialized roles commanding the highest salaries of all job categories. This rapid reshaping of salary structures, driven by AI adoption and talent shortages, makes 2024 a particularly critical moment to study compensation trends. Our project is motivated by the need to capture and understand these shifts as they unfold, providing timely insights into a labor market that is transforming faster than ever before.

title: Research Introduction: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What do you expect to find in your analysis?
text:
What do you expect to find in your analysis?
Based on the analysis of 2021 to 2023 data science compensation (Saeeji, Hayder, and Younis (2025)), we would expect a 2024 analysis to show continued overall salary growth, with the highest pay concentrated among experienced professionals, full-time employees, contractors in some cases, and senior technical roles, while salary outcomes still vary substantially by company size and location. The article also suggests that higher-paying opportunities are concentrated in select U.S. markets, with Illinois and the United States standing out in its sample, and that pay remains uneven overall, with most workers clustered in lower-to-mid salary bands and a smaller group earning very high salaries. This expectation of an uneven wage distribution is further supported by research on AI and wage inequality (Georgieff (2024)) which highlights how the rapid integration of artificial intelligence tends to widen the salary gap between high-skilled tech roles and traditional occupations. Therefore, in our analysis of the Lightcast dataset, we expect to find a distinct “AI wage premium,” where roles requiring AI competencies significantly outperform non-AI careers in average compensation. Regarding industry-specific impacts, we expect this premium to boost tech, finance, and professional services’ wages the most in 2024. These sectors rapidly adopted AI, so growth will be sharper there. Manufacturing and retail might see slower wage gains. Looking at the analysis of approximately ten million online job vacancies from 2018 to 2024 (Castaneda, Bone, and Stephany (2026)), we also expect AI-related careers in 2024 to outperform comparable non-AI roles not only in pay but also in job quality, since AI postings were associated with higher salaries and were significantly more likely to include benefits such as remote work, parental leave, and workplace-culture perks. This article also unveils trends in remote-work by showing that remote options are more common in AI postings than non-AI postings, however, it does not directly establish whether remote jobs pay more than in-office roles overall. By analyzing the Lightcast dataset, we aim to bridge this gap and clarify the intersection of remote work, location, and salary. Both articles and the OECD findings mainly support stronger pay and richer benefits for AI talent and continued location-based pay differences in high-paying U.S. markets which we look forward to validating in the Lightcast jobs dataset.

The purpose of this exploratory analysis is to understand the overall size of the dataset, salary availability, remote-work categories, geographic distribution, and industry distribution before moving into the main research analysis.

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):
    styled = df.style
    if format_dict is not None:
        styled = styled.format(format_dict)
    styled = styled.set_properties(**{
        "text-align": "left",
        "white-space": "normal",
        "border": "1px solid #ddd",
        "padding": "6px"
    }).set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th", "props": [
            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),
            ("color", "#24324b"),
            ("font-weight", "bold"),
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "8px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "td", "props": [
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "6px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "table", "props": [
            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),
            ("width", "100%")
        ]}
    ])
    return styled

This section checks salary coverage using ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED, which combines observed salary values with qualified industry-median imputed salary values. This variable is used as the main salary variable for later salary-based analysis.


Show code
salary_availability = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN salary IS NOT NULL
             AND salary > 0
        THEN 'Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Salary Missing'
    END AS salary_status,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count,
    ROUND(COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM job_postings) * 100, 2) AS job_share
FROM job_postings
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN salary IS NOT NULL
             AND salary > 0
        THEN 'Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Salary Missing'
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

    COUNT(j.id) AS job_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT j.title_clean) AS unique_job_titles,
    CONCAT_WS(', ', SLICE(COLLECT_SET(j.title_clean), 1, 10)) AS sample_titles
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN occupations o

WHERE
    o.specialized_occupation_name IS NOT NULL
    AND j.title_clean IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):
    styled = df.style
    if format_dict is not None:
        styled = styled.format(format_dict)
    styled = styled.set_properties(**{
        "text-align": "left",
        "white-space": "normal",
        "border": "1px solid #ddd",
        "padding": "6px"
    }).set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th", "props": [
            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),
            ("color", "#24324b"),
            ("font-weight", "bold"),
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "8px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "td", "props": [
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "6px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "table", "props": [
            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),
            ("width", "100%")
        ]}
    ])
    return styled

title: Exploratory Analysis
text:
This section checks salary coverage using ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED, which combines observed salary values with qualified industry-median imputed salary values. This variable is used as the main salary variable for later salary-based analysis.


Show code
salary_availability = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN salary IS NOT NULL
             AND salary > 0
        THEN 'Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Salary Missing'
    END AS salary_status,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count,
    ROUND(COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM job_postings) * 100, 2) AS job_share
FROM job_postings
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN salary IS NOT NULL
             AND salary > 0
        THEN 'Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Salary Missing'
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

}))

        "Salary Missing": "#EF553B",

    },
    title="Salary Availability After Imputation",
    labels={

title: Use Multiple Linear Regression to model salary based on location, job title, and skills.
text:
To model salary based on location, job title, and skills, we applied Multiple Linear Regression using PySpark’s ML pipeline on 30,808 job postings with valid salary data. The model used the top 20 job titles, all states, and three core skill indicators (Python, SQL, Excel) as predictors.
The model achieved an R² of 0.269, explaining approximately 27% of salary variation. The RMSE of $38,254 and MAE of $29,050 indicate that prediction errors remain substantial, suggesting that location, job title, and a limited skill set alone are insufficient to fully explain salary differences.

title: Use Multiple Linear Regression to model salary based on location, job title, and skills.
text:
To model salary based on location, job title, and skills, we applied Multiple Linear Regression using PySpark’s ML pipeline on 30,808 job postings with valid salary data. The model used the top 20 job titles, all states, and three core skill indicators (Python, SQL, Excel) as predictors.
The model achieved an R² of 0.269, explaining approximately 27% of salary variation. The RMSE of $38,254 and MAE of $29,050 indicate that prediction errors remain substantial, suggesting that location, job title, and a limited skill set alone are insufficient to fully explain salary differences.

title: Use Multiple Linear Regression to model salary based on location, job title, and skills.
text:
Salary Impact by Feature
The coefficient table shows the direction and magnitude of each feature’s relationship with salary, relative to the model’s baseline.
Job Title shows the clearest pattern: Data Analyst II, Business Intelligence Analyst, and Retail Data Analyst roles are all associated with notably lower salaries, with coefficients ranging from -$61,618 to -$75,429 relative to the baseline. This suggests entry-level and specialized analyst roles tend to pay less than the reference category.
Geographic location shows less differentiation. States such as Montana, Ohio, Tennessee, Florida, and Georgia all cluster closely together with similarly high coefficients (~$182,000–185,000), indicating that the model does not strongly distinguish salary differences across states once other factors are accounted for. The intercept of -$19,603, while not directly interpretable as a real salary value, reflects the mathematical baseline from which all coefficients are measured — actual predicted salaries are derived by combining the intercept with the relevant state, title, and skill coefficients.

title: Use Multiple Linear Regression to model salary based on location, job title, and skills.
text:
Model Limitations
With only 27% of variance explained, this baseline model indicates that job title, state, and basic technical skills (Python, SQL, Excel) capture only part of what drives salary outcomes. Important factors such as years of experience, education level, company size, and soft skills are not included in this version, which likely explains the relatively high prediction error. Expanding the feature set — for example, incorporating employment type, remote work status, and a broader range of skills — could improve model performance, as demonstrated in subsequent iterations of this analysis.

title: ML Methods - Random Forest
text:
The Random Forest model achieved an R² of 0.526 and Adjusted R² of 0.517, explaining approximately 52% of salary variation. Compared to the Multiple Linear Regression model (R² = 0.378), Random Forest improved explanatory power by nearly 15 percentage points. The lower RMSE ($30,791) and MAE ($22,192) also confirm better prediction accuracy. The minimal gap between R² and Adjusted R² indicates that the features included in the model contribute meaningfully rather than inflating performance artificially.



Unlike MLR which outputs coefficients, Random Forest provides feature importance scores that indicate how much each variable contributes to salary prediction.
The most influential variable is employment type (Full-time > 32 hours), with an importance score of 0.360 — far exceeding all other features. This suggests that whether a job is full-time or part-time has the strongest impact on salary, more so than job title or skills.
Among job titles, Data Engineer Analytics stands out as the highest-paying role (importance: 0.059), consistent with strong market demand for data engineering skills. Among skills, communication (0.085) and planning (0.076) rank highest, suggesting that soft skills play a meaningful role in salary differentiation alongside technical abilities.
Geographic location (state-level) shows relatively low importance across all states, indicating that location alone is not a strong salary predictor once job title, skills, and employment type are controlled for.



Despite the improvement over MLR, the model still leaves approximately 48% of salary variance unexplained. Key factors not available in the dataset — such as years of experience, education level, company size, and individual negotiation — likely account for the remaining variance. Future work could incorporate these variables to further improve predictive accuracy.

title: Data Preparation
text:
This page documents the data preparation process used to clean the raw Lightcast job postings dataset. The goal is to create a consistent cleaned dataset for exploratory analysis, salary analysis, regression, classification, and clustering.
The cleaning process includes checking missing values, identifying duplicate job posting IDs, reviewing suspicious salary and date values, cleaning important text and category fields, creating an analysis-ready salary variable, and saving the cleaned dataset for later pages.
Raw file: data/lightcast_job_postings.csv
Output file: data/cleaned/lightcast_cleaned.parquet



The raw Lightcast dataset contains job posting information such as job titles, companies, locations, industries, occupations, remote-work status, skills, and salary fields. I load the dataset using Spark because the file is large and the later analysis pages also rely on Spark SQL.


Show code
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col,
    count,
    round, 
    percentile_approx,
    trim,
    when,
    isnan,
    to_date,
    regexp_replace,
    sum as spark_sum
)
import os
import pandas as pd

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):
    styled = df.style
    if format_dict is not None:
        styled = styled.format(format_dict)
    styled = styled.set_properties(**{
        "text-align": "left",
        "white-space": "normal",
        "border": "1px solid #ddd",
        "padding": "6px"
    }).set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th", "props": [
            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),
            ("color", "#24324b"),
            ("font-weight", "bold"),
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "8px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "td", "props": [
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "6px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "table", "props": [
            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),
            ("width", "100%")
        ]}
    ])
    return styled

# Start Spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost") \
    .appName("SalaryCompensationTrends") \
    .getOrCreate()

spark.catalog.clearCache()

df = spark.read.option("header", "true") \
    .option("inferSchema", "true") \
    .option("multiLine", "true") \
    .option("escape", "\"") \
    .csv("data/lightcast_job_postings.csv")

df.createOrReplaceTempView("raw_jobs")

print("Original rows:", df.count())
print("Original columns:", len(df.columns))





Before cleaning, I checked missing values across all columns. This helps identify which variables have strong coverage and which fields require special handling before analysis.


Show code
total_rows = df.count()

null_counts = df.select([
    spark_sum(when(col(c).isNull(), 1).otherwise(0)).alias(c)
    for c in df.columns
]).toPandas().T.reset_index()

null_counts.columns = ["column_name", "null_count"]
null_counts["null_percent"] = (null_counts["null_count"] / total_rows * 100).round(2)

display(style_table(null_counts.sort_values("null_count", ascending=False)))





Each job posting should have a unique ID. Duplicate IDs can inflate job counts and bias downstream analysis, so I checked whether any IDs appeared more than once.


Show code
duplicate_ids = df.groupBy("ID").count().filter(col("count") > 1)

print("Duplicate job ID groups:", duplicate_ids.count())

display(style_table(duplicate_ids.limit(20).toPandas()))





Rows with missing IDs were removed because they cannot be reliably treated as unique job postings. Duplicate IDs were also removed so that each job posting appears only once in the cleaned dataset.


Show code
df_clean_base = df.filter(col("ID").isNotNull())
df_clean_base = df_clean_base.dropDuplicates(["ID"])

print("Rows after removing missing and duplicate IDs:", df_clean_base.count())





Before creating the final cleaned dataset, I reviewed salary and date fields for suspicious values. Salary values were checked for NaN values, non-positive values, unusually high values, invalid salary ranges, and unparseable posting dates.


Show code
df_check = df_clean_base.withColumn("SALARY", col("SALARY").cast("double")) \
                        .withColumn("SALARY_FROM", col("SALARY_FROM").cast("double")) \
                        .withColumn("SALARY_TO", col("SALARY_TO").cast("double"))

suspicious_values = df_check.filter(
    (col("SALARY").isNotNull() & isnan(col("SALARY")))
    | (col("SALARY") <= 0)
    | (col("SALARY") > 500000)
    | (col("SALARY_FROM") < 0)
    | (col("SALARY_TO") < 0)
    | (col("SALARY_FROM") > col("SALARY_TO"))
    | to_date(col("POSTED"), "M/d/yyyy").isNull()
)

print("Suspicious rows after removing missing IDs:", suspicious_values.count())

display(style_table(
    suspicious_values.select(
        "ID",
        "TITLE_CLEAN",
        "COMPANY_NAME",
        "SALARY",
        "SALARY_FROM",
        "SALARY_TO",
        "POSTED",
        "CITY_NAME",
        "STATE_NAME",
        "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"
    ).limit(20).toPandas()
))

checks_pd = pd.DataFrame([
    {"check": "Salary is NaN", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY").isNotNull() & isnan(col("SALARY"))).count()},
    {"check": "Salary <= 0", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY") <= 0).count()},
    {"check": "Salary > 500,000", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY") > 500000).count()},
    {"check": "Salary From < 0", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY_FROM") < 0).count()},
    {"check": "Salary To < 0", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY_TO") < 0).count()},
    {"check": "Salary From > Salary To", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY_FROM") > col("SALARY_TO")).count()},
    {"check": "Unparseable posted date", "count": df_check.filter(to_date(col("POSTED"), "M/d/yyyy").isNull()).count()},
    {"check": "Original SALARY missing", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY").isNull()).count()}
])

display(style_table(checks_pd, {
    "count": "{:,.0f}"
}))





Important text fields were trimmed to remove extra whitespace. Education values were cleaned by removing newline and carriage-return characters. Missing or [None] remote-work values were labeled as Unknown instead of being assumed to be on-site.


Show code
df_clean = df_clean_base

df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("TITLE_CLEAN", trim(col("TITLE_CLEAN")))
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("COMPANY_NAME", trim(col("COMPANY_NAME")))
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("CITY_NAME", trim(col("CITY_NAME")))
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("STATE_NAME", trim(col("STATE_NAME")))
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME", trim(col("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME")))

if "EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME" in df_clean.columns:
    df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
        "EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME",
        regexp_replace(col("EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME"), r'[\[\]"]', "")
    )

    df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
        "EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME",
        regexp_replace(col("EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME"), r"\s*,\s*", ", ")
    )

    df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
        "EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME",
        trim(
            regexp_replace(
                regexp_replace(col("EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME"), r"[\n\r]", " "),
                r"\s+",
                " "
            )
        )
    )

    df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
        "EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME",
        when(
            col("EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME").isNull() |
            (col("EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME") == "") |
            (col("EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME") == "None"),
            "Unknown"
        ).otherwise(col("EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME"))
    )

df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("SALARY", col("SALARY").cast("double"))
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("SALARY_FROM", col("SALARY_FROM").cast("double"))
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("SALARY_TO", col("SALARY_TO").cast("double"))

df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("POSTED_DATE", to_date(col("POSTED"), "M/d/yyyy"))

print("Rows after text and category cleaning:", df_clean.count())
print("Columns after text and category cleaning:", len(df_clean.columns))





Salary is a key outcome variable in this project, so I created an ANALYSIS_SALARY field using all available employer-provided salary information. If SALARY was available, it was used directly. If SALARY was missing but both SALARY_FROM and SALARY_TO were available, their midpoint was used. If only one salary bound was available, that value was used. This reduces missing salary values without replacing missing salaries with an overall median.


Show code
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
    "ANALYSIS_SALARY",
    when(
        col("SALARY").isNotNull()
        & (col("SALARY") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY") <= 500000),
        col("SALARY")
    ).when(
        col("SALARY").isNull()
        & col("SALARY_FROM").isNotNull()
        & col("SALARY_TO").isNotNull()
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY_TO") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") <= 500000)
        & (col("SALARY_TO") <= 500000)
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") <= col("SALARY_TO")),
        (col("SALARY_FROM") + col("SALARY_TO")) / 2
    ).when(
        col("SALARY").isNull()
        & col("SALARY_FROM").isNotNull()
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") <= 500000),
        col("SALARY_FROM")
    ).when(
        col("SALARY").isNull()
        & col("SALARY_TO").isNotNull()
        & (col("SALARY_TO") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY_TO") <= 500000),
        col("SALARY_TO")
    ).otherwise(None)
)

df_clean.createOrReplaceTempView("raw_jobs_clean")

print("Rows after creating ANALYSIS_SALARY:", df_clean.count())
print("Columns after creating ANALYSIS_SALARY:", len(df_clean.columns))





This section checks the original analysis salary coverage after using employer-provided salary fields. ANALYSIS_SALARY is created from SALARY, SALARY_FROM, and SALARY_TO. This is the main salary variable for primary salary analysis.


Show code
analysis_salary_check = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY IS NOT NULL
             AND ANALYSIS_SALARY > 0
        THEN 'Analysis Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Still Missing Salary'
    END AS salary_status,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count,
    ROUND(COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM raw_jobs_clean) * 100, 2) AS job_share
FROM raw_jobs_clean
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY IS NOT NULL
             AND ANALYSIS_SALARY > 0
        THEN 'Analysis Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Still Missing Salary'
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(analysis_salary_check, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}",
    "job_share": "{:.2f}%"
}))


salary_source_check = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN SALARY IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY > 0
             AND SALARY <= 500000
        THEN 'Used SALARY'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL
             AND SALARY_FROM IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY_TO IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY_FROM > 0
             AND SALARY_TO > 0
             AND SALARY_FROM <= 500000
             AND SALARY_TO <= 500000
             AND SALARY_FROM <= SALARY_TO
        THEN 'Used average of SALARY_FROM and SALARY_TO'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL
             AND SALARY_FROM IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY_FROM > 0
             AND SALARY_FROM <= 500000
        THEN 'Used SALARY_FROM only'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL
             AND SALARY_TO IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY_TO > 0
             AND SALARY_TO <= 500000
        THEN 'Used SALARY_TO only'

        ELSE 'Still missing salary'
    END AS salary_source,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN SALARY IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY > 0
             AND SALARY <= 500000
        THEN 'Used SALARY'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL
             AND SALARY_FROM IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY_TO IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY_FROM > 0
             AND SALARY_TO > 0
             AND SALARY_FROM <= 500000
             AND SALARY_TO <= 500000
             AND SALARY_FROM <= SALARY_TO
        THEN 'Used average of SALARY_FROM and SALARY_TO'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL
             AND SALARY_FROM IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY_FROM > 0
             AND SALARY_FROM <= 500000
        THEN 'Used SALARY_FROM only'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL
             AND SALARY_TO IS NOT NULL
             AND SALARY_TO > 0
             AND SALARY_TO <= 500000
        THEN 'Used SALARY_TO only'

        ELSE 'Still missing salary'
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(salary_source_check, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}"
}))


Result: After using SALARY, SALARY_FROM, and SALARY_TO, 32,394 postings have usable ANALYSIS_SALARY, while 40,082 postings remain missing salary. This original ANALYSIS_SALARY variable is used for the main salary analysis.



This step creates a separate salary variable for sensitivity analysis. Missing salaries are imputed using the median salary within the same industry only when that industry has at least 50% salary availability and at least 30 valid salary observations. The original ANALYSIS_SALARY column is not replaced.


Show code
industry_salary_quality = df_clean.groupBy("NAICS_2022_6_NAME").agg(
    count("*").alias("total_postings"),
    count(
        when(
            (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNotNull()) & (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") > 0),
            1
        )
    ).alias("salary_available_count"),
    percentile_approx(
        when(
            (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNotNull()) & (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") > 0),
            col("ANALYSIS_SALARY")
        ),
        0.5
    ).alias("industry_median_salary")
)

industry_salary_quality = industry_salary_quality.withColumn(
    "salary_available_percent",
    round(col("salary_available_count") / col("total_postings") * 100, 2)
)

qualified_industry_medians = industry_salary_quality.filter(
    (col("salary_available_percent") >= 50) &
    (col("salary_available_count") >= 30) &
    (col("industry_median_salary").isNotNull())
).select(
    "NAICS_2022_6_NAME",
    "industry_median_salary",
    "salary_available_count",
    "salary_available_percent"
)

df_clean = df_clean.join(
    qualified_industry_medians,
    on="NAICS_2022_6_NAME",
    how="left"
)

df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
    "ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED",
    when(
        (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNotNull()) & (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") > 0),
        col("ANALYSIS_SALARY")
    ).when(
        ((col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNull()) | (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") <= 0)) &
        (col("industry_median_salary").isNotNull()),
        col("industry_median_salary")
    ).otherwise(None)
)

df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
    "SALARY_IMPUTATION_STATUS",
    when(
        (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNotNull()) & (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") > 0),
        "Observed salary"
    ).when(
        ((col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNull()) | (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") <= 0)) &
        (col("industry_median_salary").isNotNull()),
        "Imputed using qualified industry median"
    ).otherwise("Still missing")
)

df_clean.createOrReplaceTempView("raw_jobs_clean")

imputation_status_check = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    SALARY_IMPUTATION_STATUS AS salary_status_after_imputation,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count,
    ROUND(COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM raw_jobs_clean) * 100, 2) AS job_share
FROM raw_jobs_clean
GROUP BY
    SALARY_IMPUTATION_STATUS
ORDER BY
    job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(imputation_status_check, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}",
    "job_share": "{:.2f}%"
}))


Result: The imputation process keeps 32,394 observed salaries unchanged and imputes 6,850 additional missing salaries using qualified industry medians. The remaining 33,232 postings still have missing salary values.



This section summarizes final salary availability after adding the industry-median imputed salary variable.


Show code
final_salary_availability_after_impute = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NOT NULL
             AND ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED > 0
        THEN 'Salary Available After Imputation'
        ELSE 'Still Missing After Imputation'
    END AS final_salary_status,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count,
    ROUND(COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM raw_jobs_clean) * 100, 2) AS job_share
FROM raw_jobs_clean
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NOT NULL
             AND ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED > 0
        THEN 'Salary Available After Imputation'
        ELSE 'Still Missing After Imputation'
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(final_salary_availability_after_impute, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}",
    "job_share": "{:.2f}%"
}))


Result: After applying qualified industry-median imputation, the usable salary sample increases from 32,394 postings to 39,244 postings. A total of 33,232 postings remain missing salary after imputation. The final salary analysis and modeling use ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED as the primary salary variable, while SALARY_IMPUTATION_STATUS is retained to distinguish observed and imputed salary records.



After industry-median imputation, some postings still have missing salary values. These are postings where no valid salary was available and the industry did not have enough salary coverage to support reliable median imputation.


Show code
missing_salary_reason_after_impute = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN SALARY IS NULL AND SALARY_FROM IS NULL AND SALARY_TO IS NULL
        THEN 'All salary fields missing'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL AND SALARY_FROM IS NULL AND SALARY_TO IS NOT NULL
        THEN 'Only SALARY_TO exists but invalid'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL AND SALARY_FROM IS NOT NULL AND SALARY_TO IS NULL
        THEN 'Only SALARY_FROM exists but invalid'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL AND SALARY_FROM IS NOT NULL AND SALARY_TO IS NOT NULL
        THEN 'Salary range exists but invalid'

        ELSE 'Other'
    END AS missing_reason,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN SALARY IS NULL AND SALARY_FROM IS NULL AND SALARY_TO IS NULL
        THEN 'All salary fields missing'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL AND SALARY_FROM IS NULL AND SALARY_TO IS NOT NULL
        THEN 'Only SALARY_TO exists but invalid'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL AND SALARY_FROM IS NOT NULL AND SALARY_TO IS NULL
        THEN 'Only SALARY_FROM exists but invalid'

        WHEN SALARY IS NULL AND SALARY_FROM IS NOT NULL AND SALARY_TO IS NOT NULL
        THEN 'Salary range exists but invalid'

        ELSE 'Other'
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(missing_salary_reason_after_impute, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}"
}))


missing_salary_by_industry_after_impute = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    NAICS2_NAME,
    COUNT(*) AS missing_salary_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
  AND NAICS2_NAME IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    NAICS2_NAME
ORDER BY
    missing_salary_count DESC
LIMIT 20
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(missing_salary_by_industry_after_impute, {
    "missing_salary_count": "{:,.0f}"
}))


missing_salary_by_state_after_impute = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    STATE_NAME,
    COUNT(*) AS missing_salary_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
  AND STATE_NAME IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    STATE_NAME
ORDER BY
    missing_salary_count DESC
LIMIT 20
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(missing_salary_by_state_after_impute, {
    "missing_salary_count": "{:,.0f}"
}))


Most remaining missing salary records still represent postings where all salary fields are missing and the posting’s industry did not meet the threshold for reliable median imputation. These postings are retained for non-salary analysis but excluded from the main salary analysis and salary prediction models.



The cleaned dataset is saved as a Parquet file. Downstream pages should load this cleaned file instead of the raw CSV.


Show code
os.makedirs("data/cleaned", exist_ok=True)

This section creates reusable prepared tables from the cleaned dataset. These tables are saved as Parquet files so that downstream pages such as salary analysis, exploratory analysis, regression, and classification can load them directly without rebuilding joins and skill tables each time.
#| echo: true #| warning: false #| message: false


Show code
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col,
    split,
    explode,
    trim,
    lit,
    regexp_replace,
    monotonically_increasing_id,
    lower,
    max as spark_max,
    when
)

os.makedirs("data/prepared", exist_ok=True)

# ----------------------------
# Companies table
# ----------------------------
companies_df = df_clean.select(
    col("COMPANY").alias("company_id"),
    col("COMPANY").alias("company"),
    col("COMPANY_NAME").alias("company_name"),

).dropDuplicates(["company_id"])

companies_df.createOrReplaceTempView("companies")

# Industries table

industries_df = df_clean.select(
    col("NAICS_2022_6").alias("naics_2022_6"),

).dropDuplicates()

industries_df = industries_df.withColumn(
    "industry_id",

)

industries_df = industries_df.select(

)

industries_df.createOrReplaceTempView("industries")

# Locations table

locations_df = df_clean.select(
    col("LOCATION").alias("location"),

    col("STATE_NAME").alias("state_name"),
    col("COUNTY_NAME").alias("county_name"),

locations_df = locations_df.withColumn(

locations_df = locations_df.select(

locations_df.createOrReplaceTempView("locations")


# ----------------------------
# Occupations table

occupations_df = df_clean.select(
    col("SOC_5").alias("soc_5"),
    col("SOC_5_NAME").alias("soc_5_name"),
    col("LOT_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION").alias("specialized_occupation"),

).dropDuplicates()

occupations_df = occupations_df.withColumn(

occupations_df = occupations_df.select(
    "occupation_id",

    "specialized_occupation",

    "occupation_group_name"
)

occupations_df.createOrReplaceTempView("occupations")


# ----------------------------
# Job postings table
# ----------------------------
job_with_industry = df_clean.join(
    industries_df,
    (
        (df_clean["NAICS_2022_6"] == industries_df["naics_2022_6"]) &
        (df_clean["NAICS_2022_6_NAME"] == industries_df["naics_2022_6_name"])
    ),
    "left"
)

job_with_location = job_with_industry.join(
    locations_df,
    (
        (job_with_industry["LOCATION"] == locations_df["location"]) &
        (job_with_industry["CITY_NAME"] == locations_df["city_name"]) &
        (job_with_industry["STATE_NAME"] == locations_df["state_name"]) &
        (job_with_industry["COUNTY_NAME"] == locations_df["county_name"]) &

job_with_keys = job_with_location.join(
    occupations_df,
    (
        (job_with_location["SOC_5"] == occupations_df["soc_5"]) &
        (job_with_location["SOC_5_NAME"] == occupations_df["soc_5_name"]) &

job_postings_df = job_with_keys.select(

    job_with_keys["SALARY_FROM"].alias("salary_from"),
    job_with_keys["SALARY_TO"].alias("salary_to"),
    job_with_keys["ANALYSIS_SALARY"].alias("observed_analysis_salary"),
    job_with_keys["ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED"].cast("double").alias("salary"),
    job_with_keys["SALARY_IMPUTATION_STATUS"].alias("salary_imputation_status"),

).filter(
    col("id").isNotNull()
).dropDuplicates(["id"])

job_postings_df.createOrReplaceTempView("job_postings")


# ----------------------------
# Skills table
# ----------------------------
def create_skill_matches(column_name, skill_type):
    return df_clean.select(
        col("ID").alias("job_id"),
        explode(split(col(column_name), ",")).alias("skill_name")
    ).withColumn(
        "skill_name",
        regexp_replace(col("skill_name"), r'[\[\]"]', "")
    ).withColumn(
        "skill_name",
        trim(col("skill_name"))
    ).withColumn(
        "skill_type",
        lit(skill_type)
    ).filter(
        col("skill_name").isNotNull() & (col("skill_name") != "")
    )

specialized_skill_matches = create_skill_matches("SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME", "specialized")
common_skill_matches = create_skill_matches("COMMON_SKILLS_NAME", "common")
software_skill_matches = create_skill_matches("SOFTWARE_SKILLS_NAME", "software")
certification_matches = create_skill_matches("CERTIFICATIONS_NAME", "certification")

job_skill_matches_raw = specialized_skill_matches.unionByName(
    common_skill_matches
).unionByName(
    software_skill_matches
).unionByName(
    certification_matches
).dropDuplicates(["job_id", "skill_name", "skill_type"])

skills_df = job_skill_matches_raw.select(
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
).dropDuplicates()

skills_df = skills_df.withColumn(
    "skill_id",
    monotonically_increasing_id()
)

skills_df = skills_df.select(
    "skill_id",
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
)

skills_df.createOrReplaceTempView("skills")

job_skill_matches_df = job_skill_matches_raw.join(
    skills_df,
    on=["skill_name", "skill_type"],
    how="left"
).select(
    "job_id",
    "skill_id",
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
)

job_skill_matches_df.createOrReplaceTempView("job_skill_matches")

# Create skill-count features for modeling
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum as spark_sum

skill_count_features_df = (
    job_skill_matches_df
    .groupBy("job_id")
    .agg(
        spark_sum(when(col("skill_type") == "common", 1).otherwise(0)).alias("common_skill_count"),
        spark_sum(when(col("skill_type") == "specialized", 1).otherwise(0)).alias("specialized_skill_count"),
        spark_sum(when(col("skill_type") == "software", 1).otherwise(0)).alias("software_skill_count")
    )
)

skill_count_features_df.createOrReplaceTempView("skill_count_features")

# ----------------------------
# AI-related job IDs
# ----------------------------
ai_job_ids = spark.sql("""
SELECT DISTINCT
    j.id AS job_id
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN job_skill_matches s
    ON j.id = s.job_id
WHERE
    LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%generative artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%mlops%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%natural language processing%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%deep learning%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%pytorch%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%artificial neural networks%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%machine learning model training%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%large language modeling%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%random forest algorithm%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%decision tree learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%ai engineer%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%ml engineer%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%generative ai%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%llm%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%generative ai%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%large language model%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%llm%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%deep learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%natural language processing%'
""")

ai_job_ids.createOrReplaceTempView("ai_job_ids")


# ----------------------------
# Jobs with AI classification
# ----------------------------
jobs_with_ai = (
    job_postings_df.alias("j")
    .join(
        ai_job_ids.alias("a"),
        col("j.id") == col("a.job_id"),
        "left"
    )
    .withColumn(
        "career_type",
        when(col("a.job_id").isNotNull(), "AI-related")
        .otherwise("Non-AI")
    )
)

jobs_with_ai.createOrReplaceTempView("jobs_with_ai")


# ----------------------------
# Job-level skill flags for modeling
# ----------------------------
skill_features_df = job_skill_matches_df.groupBy("job_id").agg(
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%python%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_python"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%sql%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_sql"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%machine learning%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_machine_learning"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%tableau%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_tableau"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%power bi%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_power_bi"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%amazon web services%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_aws"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%microsoft excel%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_excel"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%sap%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_sap")
)

skill_features_df.createOrReplaceTempView("skill_features")


# ----------------------------
# Salary regression-ready dataset
# ----------------------------
salary_regression_data = (
    job_postings_df.alias("j")
    .join(locations_df.alias("l"), col("j.location_id") == col("l.location_id"), "left")
    .join(occupations_df.alias("o"), col("j.occupation_id") == col("o.occupation_id"), "left")
    .join(skill_features_df.alias("s"), col("j.id") == col("s.job_id"), "left")
    .join(ai_job_ids.alias("a"), col("j.id") == col("a.job_id"), "left")
    .select(
        col("j.id"),
        col("j.salary").cast("double").alias("salary"),
        col("l.state_name"),
        col("l.city_name"),
        col("l.msa_name"),
        col("j.title_clean"),
        col("o.specialized_occupation_name"),
        col("o.occupation_group_name"),
        col("j.remote_type_name"),
        col("j.employment_type_name"),
        col("j.min_years_experience"),
        col("j.max_years_experience"),
        when(col("a.job_id").isNotNull(), 1).otherwise(0).alias("is_ai_related"),
        col("s.has_python"),
        col("s.has_sql"),
        col("s.has_machine_learning"),
        col("s.has_tableau"),
        col("s.has_power_bi"),
        col("s.has_aws"),
        col("s.has_excel"),
        col("s.has_sap")
    )
    .filter(
        col("salary").isNotNull() &
        (col("salary") > 0) &
        (col("salary") <= 500000)
    )
    .fillna({
        "state_name": "Unknown",
        "city_name": "Unknown",
        "msa_name": "Unknown",
        "title_clean": "Unknown",
        "specialized_occupation_name": "Unknown",
        "occupation_group_name": "Unknown",
        "remote_type_name": "Unknown",
        "employment_type_name": "Unknown",
        "min_years_experience": 0,
        "max_years_experience": 0,
        "has_python": 0,
        "has_sql": 0,
        "has_machine_learning": 0,
        "has_tableau": 0,
        "has_power_bi": 0,
        "has_aws": 0,
        "has_excel": 0,
        "has_sap": 0
    })
)

salary_regression_data.createOrReplaceTempView("salary_regression_data")


# ----------------------------
# Salary classification-ready dataset
# ----------------------------
avg_salary_value = salary_regression_data.selectExpr(
    "AVG(salary) AS avg_salary"
).collect()[0]["avg_salary"]

salary_classification_data = salary_regression_data.withColumn(
    "above_average_salary",
    when(col("salary") > avg_salary_value, 1).otherwise(0)

salary_classification_data.createOrReplaceTempView("salary_classification_data")

industries_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/industries.parquet")
locations_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/locations.parquet")
occupations_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/occupations.parquet")
